Hi there, this is Mark McCray and today we're going to talk about outsourcing. There's a lot of places we could start in this journey to building your business or consultancy, but as we take this journey, one of the most important things you will learn is the ability to leverage. I want you to think like a general, not a sergeant or a private, right from the beginning. Even if you're a one man show and need to do everything yourself, you should always be thinking about the time you will hand over non-essential tasks or low leverage tasks. Here's a basic example. For a very long time, I have not done any repairs to my house or gardening or even washed my cars. I outsource those tasks. Here's why. Let's say I earn $1,000 per hour when I consult with clients and even more when I run my businesses. When the gardener comes, it takes in two hours to do my garden. He charges me $30 per hour for a total of $60. If I were to do the garden, I would actually lose $1,940. That's because I just swapped two hours of my time that is worth $2,000 for $60. It makes no sense, right? It's the same for support, accounting, web design, SEO, PPC or any task. Ask yourself if you outsource the task and focused on your core strengths, would you make more money? Let's look at why you should outsource, what is outsourcing, how to outsource, tools to use when outsourcing, raising outsourcing fails and tips for outsourcing. Staff for both online and offline have one thing in common. They can both be a pain. You have the same problems with online and offline people. Just because someone is outsourced does not mean you don't have to manage them. Why outsource? Companies outsource to cut costs, access skilled experts, reduce overhead, get flexible staff, increase efficiency, reduce turnaround time and generate more profit. Fortune 500 companies such as IBM, Microsoft, Coca-Cola, Hewlett Packard have been outsourcing integral parts of their operation for years. It allows you to focus on the top 20% of the most profitable task. Think of the Pareto principle, the 80-20 rule. It's more productive, motion does not equal results, and a conventional office, there's a lot of movement without results. Work is no longer a place. Think of how mobile you have become. It can free up time and allow you more freedom from the office. When you have staff in an office, you feel obliged to go there. It helps create a culture of accountability. Ultimately, business is made up of systems for managing people and the mechanics of the business. That can happen anywhere. Think Nike. Nike outsources all, most all of its production. It focuses on its core competencies. Pre-production, research and development, post-production, marketing, distribution and sales. It is the number one seller of athletic shoes in the world, yet it outsources making the shoes. Over 90% of Fortune 500 companies outsource, the outsource radiologist. Highly skilled professions are changing the way they work. In the US, radiologists are centralizing. One company has created a hub that operates 24 hours, seven days a week, 365 days a year, where radiologists from around the world can send in their x-rays and get better and faster results than using their own in-house or local radiologists. Employee Utilization. A common key performance indicator for large organization is employee utilization. If someone is employed to work 40 hours per week, they still may only be productive 30 hours per week. In other words, 75% of the time. Realistically, that is probably 10 hours out of 40. If someone is productive 25% of the time, you're lucky. Employees need breaks, sick leave, social interaction to name a few. The outsource model is always 100% utilization of what you want it to be. Outsources are only paid by the hour for what they do. The clock stops if they do. Outsourcing is a very productive model. If you can teach someone a task that costs you $5 but produces $10 of income, how many people would you hire? This should get your attention. This is an example of a company with 20 employees working 38 hours per week at $25 per hour. 20 employees times 38 hours equals 760 hours per week. 52 weeks equals 39,520 hours per year. 39,520 hours at $25 per hour equals $988,000. Outsource, that number could be $39,520 at $5 per hour or $197,600 per year as a savings of $790,400 or 80%. Let's assume a more realistic number of say 50% outsourced, that would still be $395,200 in savings. That could mean the difference between making a profit and going out of business. Some things I have outsourced, programming, SEO expert, email expert, transcriptionist, expert webmaster, support assistant, CMS errors and updates, virtual assistants and executive assistant, Facebook expert, custom plugin, Jumla bug fixes, business processes such as Kaizana experts modeling, WordPress themes, Google analytics, designers, top level management, statistical analysis, superstar telemarketer, social media manager, computer teacher, English teacher, voice talent, traffic builder, link builder, copywriters, video scribing, web and print designers. So what is outsourcing? It's finding people outside of your company who are not generally employed by you that could perform a job you would normally hire someone to do. If you have a member of your staff that uses a computer as the main part of his or her job, the job can probably be outsourced. Different types of outsourcing. Offshoring. Jobs are outsourced overseas like customer service. On-site outsourcing. Jobs that cannot be outsourced overseas like security services. Common outsourced areas, accounting and financial, preparing financial statements, general billing, functions, payroll, processing. Customer service, routine support, calls, emails, live chats. Distribution and fulfillment services, companies that pack and ship your products to customers or to other branches, human resources and insurance administration. Good for companies with high turnover. Information technology, technical support, programmers and webmasters that can perform functions you do not have internal expertise for. Marketing, advertising, sales marketing, telemarketing, network and telecommunications, secretarial, scheduling, confirming appointments and making travel arrangements and back office operations. Online security, transcription, converting audio or video into written format. I outsource a lot of voice talent work for different businesses in different countries I need different accents. Hi there, thank you for taking the time to request more information. We don't want to lose you so please take a minute to make sure you have received the very important email we just sent. I outsource video scribing. It takes 22 hours for one minute of video scribing and costs $3,000 per minute. Outsource costs $750 per minute. Meet Bob. Bob's a smart guy. He works as a junior software designer for a little company you may have heard of started by a guy named Bill. I outsource technical work. When I wanted to improve the processes in my companies, I looked at hiring Kaisan Six Sigma and process modeling experts to find the right one. I hired 30 contractors simultaneously. Every person I hire is tested first. Case study number one coming to Australia. I hired an Australian contractor to find out information for me before I came to Australia, including best internet connection and where to get it, cost of living, cost of rent, top three seven-seater cars, by cost and depreciation, top schools, by academic results in Queensland. The point was that I didn't want to have any downtime. So when I arrived in Australia, I got off the plane, went to the hotel and waiting for me on my iPhone was instructions and a street map of where to go to collect my new internet connection. And the total downtime was about from arriving in the country to getting to the hotel and being connected again was three to four hours, all because I had used someone, an outsourced person, to do most of the research for me. Case study number two was a conference business. I used to own a conference business. So I needed to know where all of the conference venues were in that country. So I outsourced someone to find me the details of every conference venue in the country. That cost $500. I started with a regular full-time employee, then I switched to a contractor from the Philippines who had done tele sales. The Philippines outsourced person was 50% of the price and 100% more productive as measured by results, number of people called and number of deals closed. We used high-rise CRM software where we could get street views and we can even research whether in traffic before the call. Case study number three, marketing for the financial vertical. I came up with the plans and strategies. Those plans and strategies get passed to my team to execute. All team members are outsourced and I have only ever met three people on the team. There are between 15 to 30 people on the team at any one time. All operations are outsourced. Copywriting is done in the USA. Editing is done in Serbia. Website design and DVD design done in India. Photography done in Canada. Merchant services provided in USA and product fulfillment is done in the USA. For example, we made a professional documentary. It was scripted in the USA, filmed in Canada, post-production and creation edited in Serbia. The world is a village and technology allows you to reach out to anywhere. My point is that you can do some very complicated things all without source people. Case study number four, outsourcing the sales funnel. We have 5,000 new leads per month. We mail five days a week. Average total leads that we have is 150,000. That means that 750,000 mailing pieces go out a week. We have a full-time team of 15 people working 24-7 to achieve that. The front end is often a daily newsletter. For this to work, I need accurate economic news from experts, designers, traders, admin, person and copywriters. The outsource way of life. I have an office I never go to. That office costs $80 per month, but I have the use of a boardroom. Someone picks up the telephone for me. There's a mailing service. I can fax, scan, photocopying. There's an internet desk there. If I want to go and work there, I can. I have the option of using the administration and personal assistant services if I choose. In the last few years, I've probably been once or twice. Outsource model number one, third-party outsource companies. This would be my first recommendation for people new to outsourcing, especially if you're an established company. There are companies in most countries that you can go to and they will arrange your outsource requirements for you. They will, for example, help you outsource your entire accounting team or part of your business. They are team focused. They might, for example, have an established outsourced and account team already in place that you can plug into. A friend I know who specializes in this told me that 53% of companies that try to outsource themselves fail. Outsource model two, DIY outsourcing. With this model, you do the outsourcing yourself. This will suit startups and one-man shows. You might want to hire someone to handle your emails, make appointments, or even find your leads. How we hire? How you hire is the secret of outsourcing and where most people fail. Spend as much time on this step as you can. Step one, different jobs have different processes. In this example, I was looking for someone who could help improve our email process. We test how well their spoken English is. We want to hear how good their internet connection is. We need to know how good they are at what they do. So, for example, in this test, we sent them to thebbc.com. We asked them to pick an article and then record themselves reading that article. Then upload the recording so that we could hear how they sounded. Then step two was to get them to write their 400-word letter explaining why they thought process was important in the company and then to include any reports that they've done previously. Now, the idea of this when you do this is you want to hear, if you're going to communicate with this person on a daily basis, you want to know that you can communicate with them fluently in English. You want to know that their internet connection is good enough for you to have a conversation. You want to know, depending on what job it is, if they do telly sales, for example, you want to know that they have a crystal clear line and what the background noises are. The fact that they have to upload something gives you a kind of idea of their skill level. Step two, relevant tests. Whatever the position is, we always give them a test. So, if they are bookkeeping, accounting, or data inputting, we might give them an Excel test and that can be basic to very advanced. If it's English or support, we might give them an English test and sometimes a proofing test. We have a range of different tests depending on what the job is. So, whatever you want to hire someone as, give them a test. Make sure that they can do it. You can either do the test for free or pay them, you know, whatever it is, an hour or two hour of work. It's far better to pay for that test than employ someone just because they got good reviews and you don't really know if they're any good. Step three, again, it depends on the type of person and what job you're hiring them for, but for a higher level management position, we will put them through a fairly lengthy and in-depth interview. And one of the interview processes that I like, I got from a book called Top Grading. And there is an interview process and questions in there. By the time you've finished that, you know, you're fairly confident you know that person. If it's really a high level person, then step four, we'll put them through a personality test or a personality profile. And these profiles I find to be very accurate. For example, one of the ones that I use is called instinctive drives. It costs about $100 per person to put through. And if you have volume, I think it's $50. Not 100% sure of the price, but it's inexpensive. And it measures four parts of the personality, whether you're a verifier or an authenticator or a completer or you like to improvise. So normally in a company, you don't have enough completers, people who actually get things done. And if you're in a creative environment, you might have people who score very highly in improvising. So it depends what you're looking for. But between the interview and something like this, you can really get close to what that person is like. The galates, with all contractors, I suggest that you get them to sign some kind of document. We have all of our contractors are asked to sign non disclosure agreements. Contractors must also agree to relinquish all ownership to any intellectual property. And we have a copy of their ID documents for everyone who works for us. I'm going to cover this more in the process section. But as soon as they actually are taken on board, they generally don't start work for a week, we put them through a whole process of onboarding, which I'll cover in another lesson. Double blind system. For example, ODesk. If the contractor leaves or is fired, you leave feedback for them. They don't see that for 14 days, and they leave feedback for you that you don't see immediately either. You have a kind of dashboard in most of the outsource companies. And you can see the number of contractors who have been employed by me and given me a review, the hours I have been billed for, and the number of contractors I have hired, they're fairly standard throughout the different companies. And most of the outsource platforms, you will find that they have some kind of monitoring where the company, the intermediary, the third party has software on the contractors computer where you can see what they're doing during the time that you have hired them for. So if you have hired them from an hour or two hours to do something, you can see in their work diary what they did during that time. Outsourcing websites, ELANCE is one of the older ones, the pay by the hour or project, work there's a workroom, which allows you to track the work in progress. Freelancer.com, they pay based on milestones or complete work. So free sign up, but take note of other fees. Some of these have different fees. So you just got to make sure what the fees are involved with the company that you're using. ODesk, that changed its name to Upwork for some unknown reason, is one of the bigger ones and one that I use a lot. You pay by the hour or the project and ODesk takes 10% fee. So for example, if you were going to pay somebody $5 per hour, you would be charged, I think it's $5.56 or wherever it is for the contractor to receive $5 and for ODesk to receive its fee, they charge you a bit. There's no other fees that I'm aware of, so that's fairly straightforward. Guru, you pay for completed work. Get a freelancer. Here you can post your project for programmers to bid on. Get a coder. Here you can get a service for any technical programming, web design and other website related work. Outsourcing tools that we use, and this changes and evolves over time, but as Santa is one that we use a lot, it provides a great way for teams to communicate and collaborate with one another. All files sync automatically, so the team members don't need to keep using email or phoning each other. Communication and Santa is focused on tasks, so work is done faster and in an organized manner. Basecamp, we've used basecamp for a long time. It's good if you're a smaller business and you have under 40 projects. It's easy to work with. It's easier for your customers to get to know how to work. It's very intuitive and it's relatively inexpensive. I think it's about $50 a month. Adlasian, we've used it for a while. I'm kind of moving off it right now, but it's still a good tool. We use it for a kind of Wikipedia where we can put all of our knowledge and all of the company's sort of intellectual property and it's a place where we can put employees through a process to onboard them. Go to webinar. We use daily and you can have up to a thousand people in a webinar. You can see each other on camera. You can see each other's screen, so it's good to work with collaboratively. I think they have a smaller one called GoToMeeting, which we also use, which is designed for, I think, is 25 people are under. Skype, of course. Most people have Skype now. Our company lives and dies by Skype. Everybody in our team has that on during their workday. Here, we'll give you an idea of the size of the outsource industry. These are a few years old. Odesk, for example, had $920 million in freelancer earnings in that year. That was bigger than Elancer. Freelancer are 99 designs at the time. Tips. Don't focus on your weaknesses. Focus on your strengths and outsource your weaknesses. Think Nike. Have a process in place before outsourcing the particular task or operation. Vet outsource contractors or partners thoroughly. Review past employers' feedback. In Odesk, you can view it on the contractor's profile under Work History and Feedback section. Reason outsourcing fails. The number one reason companies fail to get the desired result from outsourcing is that they have no process in place to teach the contractor. The second most common reason is lack of communication with the contractor. The third most common reason is the employer doesn't have a well thought out plan. The contractor will often outperform the job specification and constantly wait for work. So smart objectives make your project measurable, achievable, realistic, time-scaled and be very specific. Follow up based on milestones you have previously set. In situations where your company will be exposed to significant risk, consult an experienced advisor before outsourcing. Now this is interesting. This presentation that you just saw, I first gave a version of this in 2013 to a group of CEOs in Australia. And to make the presentation, I had this conversation with one of my outsources and her name was Mars. So I said, Mars, I want you to make me a report. I have a presentation to do in two weeks about the benefits of outsourcing. It should include why outsource examples of outsourcing, where to outsource, some of our own case studies. I'm thinking of 20 slide PowerPoint. And the last slide put a pick of you with the words, this presentation was outsourced. This is Mars. This is put together, this presentation. That's it guys. I hope you've enjoyed the presentation and just leave me a comment if you have any questions.
